forecasts.reasoning: The border closures initiated by Benin on May 7, 2025, were driven by serious national security concerns regarding arms smuggling and a new militant group, "Mahmuda." Rather than de-escalating, the initial closures in Kwara State were quickly followed by extensions of the border clampdown to Lagos State (May 10) and waterway routes in Ogun State (May 21). This pattern indicates a widening security operation by the Benin government. Historically, border closures in West Africa (especially over national security) typically take many months to resolve, as seen with Nigeria's 16-month closure in 2019-2020 and the lengthy Benin-Niger blockade in 2023-2024. With the knowledge cutoff at May 31, 2025, there is only a 1-month window before the July 1, 2025 deadline. Given the lack of any reports regarding diplomatic breakthroughs and the recency of the escalations, a total reversal and reopening within 30 days is exceedingly unlikely.

In late May 2025 (days before the cutoff date), French President Macron publicly pushed for European partners, specifically lobbying the UK, Netherlands, and Belgium, to join France in recognizing the State of Palestine at an upcoming UN conference on the two-state solution. This conference is firmly scheduled for June 2025 (specifically June 17-20 in New York, organized alongside Saudi Arabia). Macron has explicitly stated that the conference will "give fresh impetus to the recognition." Given the strong momentum, the formal scheduling of the conference as a target action-date, and the concerted diplomatic pressure from France upon several European states, the probability of at least one UN member country officially announcing its recognition of Palestine in June 2025 is very high. While there's a small chance of delay due to US/Israeli pressure or diplomatic bottlenecks, the broad coalition and public commitments make it highly probable.
